You're not apt to want to get on the phone because you're dealing with voicemail, you're trying to get a hold of somebody, and even if you get a hold of somebody, most companies these days are directing you to HR and they're just going to give you a name-ranking serial number. You name the convention code. But that's something that companies don't want to do and we kind of do that just to make ourselves a one-stop shop. Well, the health thing sort of answers it that what bothered me the most, I mean I don't like the, I don't like your news format and then click to get the articles at all. Having that available is okay but this thing needs a front on it. That is the letter of the month from you and has some, you know, it needs to be more news lettery. But the biggest thing I'd say to you is that there's your handicap by being generic. And so I think I said it during the two days. But you know, so like if you're at our events, you know, at every event there's the, but my business is different sign and we always talk about my business is different. And in a sense we have no choice. We have no niche bias big enough to niche. But so we're always fighting an uphill battle with that. Whereas in reality the most effective thing you could do would be to accept it and pander to it. So the problem with being generic here is that your healthcare client arrives at the website or gets your employer's guide to workplace drug and alcohol testing or any of these sorts of things. They have a very easy opportunity to reject it has not for them. Because it doesn't say it's for them. And the minute you got a story in it about an airline, Mary at the nursing home can too easily, oh this isn't for us. This is for big companies. We're not a big company or we're not an airline or we whatever. So you and the opposite the other side of that coin is the real opportunity is in pandering to it. So the minute Mary sees it and it says, you know, the assisted living and home care services employer's guide to workplace drug and alcohol testing. And that's for me reaction. And not only is the problem gone away, but she's more receptive to you. And the reason why most companies and info marketers refuse to do it is pure and simple laziness staff, infrastructure, etc. Because running five vertical businesses under one roof is more complicated than one run on one big marketing funnel everybody gets the same shit. So you may not have five niches that justify it. But if you got one, and if it's healthcare, then where are you I'd have two businesses. I'd have my generic, and I'd have my healthcare. And I would tweak everything I was doing to be healthcare only. So all the stories are healthcare is all the testimonials are healthcare. All the examples are healthcare. The thing says healthcare all over it. And if you want, you can get price differential. Because the minute you specialize it, even though it is not but the minute it's perceived as specialized, its price can go up. And so you really handicap yourself. So like in the theft control business with Jackson, he started as employee and deliver man theft for supermarket convenience stores and drug drug stores. The very first thing I did was splinter it. So we had three industries, and we had stuff for three industries. And we talked to the three industries in their language and the drug store guys only got drug store testimonials and the supermarket. Now we mixed them at the seminars, by the way. So once it got to the point of them coming to the public seminars when he wasn't brought in by a corporation, we mixed them in the room. The domain of difference. But until they got there, they didn't realize there was anybody there except drug store guys. Now the audio cassette programs, for example, there was no difference at all to the product. No supplement, no change, no nothing. So the audio cassettes used examples from all three industries. All right, mixed bag. All we did is change the title and the cover. But so it appeared to you to be all about you. And it allowed for dramatic improvements in pricing. So the biggest thing I would say to you is if there's only one fine, if there's two that are significant and significantly better than others, then I would create two, you know, completely different, different niches. Yeah. I mean, if I was still really doing what we were once doing in chiropractic and dentistry, I'd have chiro and dental versions of everything I do. There'd be very little differences in them. But I wouldn't have no BS time management for entrepreneurs and I've had no BS time management for entrepreneurial chiropractors. I mean, and I'd go through and plunk chiropractor in, change some of the stories and change some of the testimonies. I double the price. So you're saying you can keep the parent company and just wonder if... Well, yeah, you have to have a generic for all the mixed bag guys, right? So you'd like, you don't have enough carpet cleaning companies to justify doing anything for just carpet cleaning guys. And you don't have enough info marketers to justify doing anything for info marketers. But where it's a really significant part of your business and could be even better. If it's one, then just so you got your generic business, pretty much exactly like it is now. And then you got a...
